1: The tradition of the elders (paradosis ton presbyteron) in Mark's context requires the unusual explanation in vv.3-4 that all Jews wash their hands before eating and observe other purity practices — explanation unnecessary for Jewish readers but clearly aimed at Gentile ones. The corban controversy (v.11) targets a legal device by which property could be dedicated to the temple (making it formally unavailable to others) without actually being delivered — a loophole that allowed children to evade the fifth commandment's financial implications toward aged parents. The Mishnah confirms corban as a legal category (m. Nedarim).
2: Ritual hand-washing before meals was not a Mosaic commandment but a tradition extending priestly purity requirements (Exod 30:19-21) to daily life — a Pharisaic innovation that treated ordinary eating as a quasi-priestly act. By the first century this practice was a marker of Pharisaic piety, later codified in the Mishnah tractate Yadayim (Hands). The word translated "defiled" (koinais, common/ordinary) renders Hebrew tame — hands not ceremonially washed were "common," unprepared for a sacred act. Mark's explanation addresses Gentile readers who would have had no context for the observance.
3: This verse is one of Mark's several parenthetical explanations of Jewish customs inserted for his Gentile Roman audience (see also 7:11, 14:12). The phrase "holding to the tradition of the elders" (kratountes ten paradosin ton presbyteron) identifies the practice as oral tradition — what Josephus calls the Pharisees' "laws from their fathers" (Ant. 13.297), later formalized in the Mishnah as the Oral Torah. Mark's claim that "all the Jews" observe this practice is a simplification; hand-washing was primarily a Pharisaic and priestly-aspirant practice, not universal across social classes.
4: The phrase "when they come from the marketplace" (apo agoras) reflects a Pharisaic concern about incidental contact with Gentiles and their ritual impurity in public commercial space. Levitical law did not require hand-washing after marketplace visits, but Pharisaic interpretation extended purity concerns to any environment where impurity might be contracted. The additional list — washing cups, pitchers, kettles, and dining couches — is ethnographically accurate: the Mishnah specifies washing requirements for stone and metal vessels, since clay vessels contaminated by contact could not be purified (Lev 11:33). Mark's account is factual observation, not caricature.
5: The formal question comes from a delegation including Jerusalem-based scribes (7:1) — not local critics but representatives of the central religious establishment. The phrase "tradition of the elders" (paradosis ton presbyteron) would have been immediately understood by any Jew as a reference to oral law, the corpus of interpretive tradition the Pharisees believed to have been given alongside written Torah at Sinai. The disciples' non-observance of this tradition would have been treated as a serious social and legal failure in Pharisaic circles, not a minor eccentricity.
6: Jesus cites Isaiah 29:13 from the LXX, the Greek translation familiar to Mark's audience. Isaiah 29 addresses Jerusalem's leaders who perform religious duties outwardly while their inward commitment has drifted from YHWH. The term "hypocrites" (hypokritai) was originally theatrical — an actor playing a role, whose outward performance does not match inner reality. In Second Temple moral discourse, the concept of the divided person (outward conformity, inward distance) was a recognized failure mode that wisdom and prophetic texts repeatedly condemned. Jesus applies the term not to all Pharisees but to those who substitute external ritual compliance for genuine covenant loyalty.
7: The second half of the Isaiah 29:13 citation distinguishes genuine worship from religious activity driven by "commands taught by humans" (entalmata anthropon). In Isaiah's context, these were syncretistic innovations adopted from surrounding cultures; Jesus applies the same critique to oral tradition deployed to nullify written Torah obligations. The critique is not of tradition per se but of tradition used to circumvent rather than fulfill the intent of Scripture — a distinction later rabbinic discussion also took seriously in its debates about how oral and written Torah relate.
8: The contrast between "commands of God" (entolen tou theou) and "human traditions" (paradosin ton anthropon) is not a rejection of interpretation or tradition but a hierarchy claim: when interpretive tradition conflicts with written command, the written command takes precedence. This was a live debate within first-century Judaism — the Sadducees, who accepted only written Torah and rejected Pharisaic oral tradition, would have agreed with Jesus on the principle even if not with his conclusions. The Pharisees themselves debated which traditions were binding and which were discretionary.
9: The ironic phrase "you have a fine way" (kalos athetete) — literally "you do well to set aside" — is rhetorical sarcasm characteristic of Hebrew prophetic disputation. Jesus grants the Pharisees credit for thoroughness in doing exactly what he is condemning: they are consistent and successful at setting aside God's commands in order to maintain their own system. The construction sharpens the critique by suggesting the pattern is not incidental error but systematic policy.
10: Moses's command "Honor your father and mother" is the fifth commandment (Exod 20:12; Deut 5:16), the first with a promise attached and the one that established the framework for family obligation in a society without social welfare institutions. The accompanying death penalty for cursing parents (Exod 21:17; Lev 20:9) indicates how seriously Torah treated this obligation. Elderly parents in the ancient world depended entirely on adult children for material support; the commandment was not merely an ethical ideal but an operative legal framework for elder care.
11: Korban (Aramaic and Hebrew qorban) was a dedicatory formula by which a person declared property "an offering to God" — formally consecrating it to the temple treasury. Once declared korban, the property was legally removed from ordinary use, binding the owner by a vow. Crucially, the property did not have to be immediately delivered to the temple; it could remain in the owner's possession while being formally unavailable for other purposes. The Mishnah tractate Nedarim (Vows) discusses corban extensively, including cases where a son uses the formula specifically to avoid supporting his parents — a concern rabbinic courts ultimately addressed by allowing annulment of such vows in extreme cases.
12: The practical effect of the corban declaration was that a son could declare his resources "devoted to God" and thereby legally exempt himself from supporting aged parents, while retaining actual use of those resources for himself. The temple benefited in theory (the property was formally consecrated) but not in practice; parents lost their legal claim. Jesus identifies this as a mechanism that produces real material harm to vulnerable people while hiding behind religious language. The critique is not abstract theology but concrete social ethics: the legal device fails the very people Scripture most explicitly protects.
13: "Nullifying" (akyrountes) is a legal term: to make void, to deprive of operative force. Jesus's accusation is that oral tradition has been deployed not to interpret written Torah but to override it — stripping the fifth commandment of its legal force through a recognized workaround. The phrase "and many things like that" generalizes the critique: corban is not an isolated case but a specimen of a pattern. This conclusion formally closes the purity dispute initiated by the Jerusalem scribes in 7:1, and Jesus has now counter-charged them with a Torah violation far more serious than the one they raised against his disciples.
14: Jesus's turn from the Pharisees to the crowd is a deliberate rhetorical move that takes the dispute into the public domain. The double imperative "Listen to me, everyone, and understand" (Akousate mou pantes kai synete) echoes the Shema tradition and the wisdom calls of Proverbs 8 — this is addressed to all Israel, not just the religious establishment. What follows in v.15 is presented as universally accessible teaching, in contrast to the private explanation reserved for disciples in v.17.
15: The saying "nothing outside a person can defile them by going into them" overturns the operative logic of the Levitical food system. Leviticus 11 distinguishes clean and unclean animals; Leviticus 15 addresses bodily discharges; Numbers 19 addresses corpse impurity — all involve external sources of defilement transmitted by contact or ingestion. Jesus's aphorism does not merely prioritize moral purity over ritual purity; it reassigns the causal mechanism of defilement from external contact to internal moral generation. Mark's editorial note in v.19 ("declaring all foods clean") confirms he understood the saying as having precisely that radical implication for his community's food practices.
16: This verse ("He who has ears to hear, let him hear") is absent from the oldest manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and is likely a scribal addition by analogy with 4:9 and 4:23. If secondary, it reflects early readers' perception that 7:14-15 was a decisive pronouncement requiring the same attentive response as the parable discourse. Even as an addition, it accurately signals the theological weight of the preceding statement.
17: The movement from public crowd teaching to private house instruction follows Mark's consistent pattern: public parable, then private explanation to disciples (4:10-20; 4:34). The house (oikos) functions as the site of authorized interpretive access; the crowd receives the aphorism, the disciples receive its meaning. This two-tier structure resembles the Qumran community's distinction between public teaching and the pesher (interpretation) reserved for the initiated, and later rabbinic patterns of public versus academy-level teaching.
18: Jesus's question "Are you so dull?" (Houtos kai hymeis asynchetoi este) is a Socratic teaching provocation signaling that the correct inference should already be accessible from what the disciples have seen and heard. Mark's sustained attention to the disciples' incomprehension (4:13; 6:52; 8:17-21) is a distinctive pastoral feature of the Gospel: if even the twelve who traveled with Jesus struggled to understand, the community's own struggles with comprehension are not evidence of abandonment but of a shared condition that only post-resurrection illumination would resolve.
19: The parenthetical "In saying this, Jesus declared all foods clean" (katharizōn panta ta bromata) is Mark's editorial interpretation, absent from Matthew's parallel. This is among the most consequential editorial glosses in the Gospels. For Mark's Roman Gentile audience navigating questions about Jewish food laws in mid-first-century communities (cf. Rom 14-15; Acts 10:9-16; 1 Cor 8), the declaration settles a live controversy. The physiological explanation that follows — food enters the stomach, not the heart — reflects ancient medical anthropology: the heart (kardia) was the organ of moral reasoning, the stomach (koilia) the organ of digestion. The two systems have no moral interaction.
20: This verse transitions from the physiological aside to the positive anthropology of defilement. The structure mirrors prophetic moral discourse in Ezekiel and Jeremiah: the problem of Israel is not external contamination but the condition of the heart (kardia). In Hebrew anthropology, the heart (lev) was the seat of cognition, will, and moral character — not merely emotion. What comes out of the heart represents what the person has been generating inwardly as a matter of character, not an external force acting on a neutral self.
21: The catalogue of vices that follows (vv.21-22) has formal parallels in Hellenistic moral philosophy (Stoic and Epicurean vice lists) and Jewish texts (Wisd. Sol. 14:25-26; 1QS 4:9-11 from the Dead Sea Scrolls). Such lists were a recognized pedagogical tool for moral education in both traditions. The list is organized to cover major categories of social harm: sexual violations, property crimes, physical violence, and relational-reputational damage. "Evil thoughts" (dialogismoi kakoi) head the list — the heart generates intentions that then express themselves in specific acts.
22: Several terms carry specific cultural weight. "Slander" (blasphemia) in this context means damaging speech about a neighbor, not only speech against God. "Arrogance" (hyperephania) — treating others as inferiors — was a vice particularly condemned in Jewish wisdom literature (Prov 16:5; Sir 10:7) and in the honor-shame culture of the ancient Mediterranean, where claiming unearned honor at others' expense was a recognized social harm. "Folly" (aphrosyne) is not mere stupidity but the willful moral blindness that Proverbs consistently contrasts with wisdom.
23: The summary "all these evils come from inside" formally closes the purity controversy that began in 7:1. Jesus has refuted the initial charge, cited Scripture, identified the Pharisees' legal maneuver, and now articulated a positive anthropology of defilement that replaces the external Levitical category with an internal moral one. The concluding summary also frames what immediately follows: the Gentile encounters of 7:24-8:10 demonstrate that Gentile birth and ethnic origin produce no moral defilement — the heart, not genealogy or geography, is the determinative category.
24: Tyre was an ancient Phoenician city-state on the Mediterranean coast (modern Lebanon), approximately 50-60 km northwest of Capernaum. A major commercial center with a long Hebrew Bible history — trading partner of Solomon (1 Kgs 5) and target of prophetic judgment (Isa 23; Ezek 26-28; Amos 1:9-10) — it was predominantly Gentile. Jesus entering Gentile territory immediately after declaring that Gentile birth produces no moral defilement (7:19-23) is narratively deliberate: the theological declaration and the geographic crossing are coordinated moves.
25: The woman who "fell at his feet" (prosepesen pros tous podas autou) performed an act of petitionary prostration standard in Hellenistic culture when appealing to a person of higher status or authority — not worship but a recognized posture of supplication that acknowledged the petitioner's lower position. The detail about her daughter being possessed by an "unclean spirit" (pneumati akathartō) uses Mark's standard vocabulary for demonization (cf. 1:23; 3:11; 5:2).
26: Mark gives the woman a precise ethnic identification: "a Greek, born in Syrian Phoenicia" (Hellenis, Syrophoinikissa to genei). "Greek" here means Hellenized — Greek-speaking and Greek-cultured — rather than ethnically from Greece. "Syrian Phoenicia" distinguished the coastal region from Libyo-Phoenicia in North Africa. This double ethnic notation removes all ambiguity: she is outside the Jewish covenant community by birth, language, and culture. The precision makes the encounter a test case for whether Jesus's healing authority extends across the ethnic boundary his teaching in 7:15-23 has just redrawn.
27: Jesus's reply "Let the children first be fed" (aphes proton chortasthenai ta tekna) uses proton — first, not only or never. The Israel-first, Gentiles-second priority sequence was standard in Paul's articulation of gospel logic ("to the Jew first and also to the Greek," Rom 1:16) and reflects the actual historical sequence of Jesus's mission and the early church. The metaphor of "dogs" (kynariois, diminutive — household pets rather than scavenging street dogs) was a standard Jewish idiom for Gentiles, used here not as a terminal verdict but as the premise the woman's counterargument will turn.
28: The woman's response — "even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs" — accepts the metaphor and reframes it: she does not dispute Israel's priority but argues that household dogs are present at the table and receive incidental overflow. The argument is not about overthrowing the mission order but about the natural spill of grace beyond its primary recipients. The Greek hypokato tes trapezes (under the table) is a precise household image: small domestic dogs (kynaria) in Hellenistic homes gathered near the dining table during meals, a common and recognizable domestic scene.
29: "For such a reply" (Dia touton ton logon) — literally "because of this word/argument." Jesus grants the request on the basis of the quality of the woman's theological reasoning, not merely her persistence. This is the only Markan healing accomplished at a distance (cf. the centurion's servant in Matt 8:13), which makes it a demonstration of authority transcending physical presence and ethnic boundary simultaneously. The remote exorcism across ethnic lines is the narrative enactment of the theological principle Jesus articulated in 7:15-23.
30: The discovery of healing upon returning home completes the episode. Mark's detail "lying on the bed, and the demon gone" (epi ten klinen beblemenon kai to daimonion exelēlythos) is precise: the child was lying quietly — the violent restlessness associated with demonization (cf. 1:26; 9:26) was absent. The brief closure verse implicitly argues that the same healing power active in Jewish Galilee operates without diminution across ethnic and geographic boundaries, substantiating what Jesus declared in the preceding controversy.
31: The route Mark describes — from Tyre through Sidon, then south through the Decapolis to the Sea of Galilee — is geographically circuitous, going north before turning south and east. Various explanations have been offered: Mark may reflect actual itinerant ministry through regions where Jesus worked, or the route emphasizes the extent of the Gentile circuit. The Decapolis, a Roman-sponsored league of Hellenistic cities east of the Jordan, was predominantly Gentile territory — confirming that this healing, like the Syrophoenician episode, takes place outside Jewish territory.
32: The deaf man with a speech impediment (mogilalon, one who speaks with difficulty) is brought by "some people" who beg (parakalousi) Jesus to lay his hand on him — collective intercession on behalf of a vulnerable person unable to advocate for himself. The petition for hand-laying was a recognized form of healing invocation in Jewish tradition; rabbis and holy men laid hands on the sick as a form of blessing (cf. 5:23; 8:23-25). The collective nature of the intercession echoes the Gerasene episode (5:1-20) where a community brought its problem to Jesus.
33: Jesus's healing technique — taking the man aside privately, inserting fingers in his ears, spitting and touching his tongue — has parallels in Greco-Roman healing practices and in Jewish traditions of holy-man healing. Saliva was understood in antiquity as a medium carrying something of the healer's vital force; both Tacitus (Hist. 4.81) and Suetonius (Vesp. 7) record the emperor Vespasian using saliva to heal. The private withdrawal from the crowd follows the Messianic Secret pattern (1:44; 5:43; 8:26). The physical actions also communicate with the deaf man nonverbally — Jesus demonstrates what he intends to do before doing it.
34: Ephphatha is one of several Aramaic words Mark preserves with translation for his Greek-speaking audience (cf. talitha koum 5:41; Boanerges 3:17; Eloi Eloi lama sabachthani 15:34; Abba 14:36). The consistent preservation of Aramaic originals in a Greek text suggests a tradition that valued the actual words of Jesus as carrying authority in themselves — not mere translation equivalents. Looking up to heaven before speaking was a standard Jewish posture of prayer; the "deep sigh" (estenaxen) suggests intercessory groaning analogous to prophetic intercession, the same physical response as at the tomb of Lazarus (John 11:33, 38).
35: The immediate healing (eutheōs, Mark's characteristic urgency-word) is described in precise physiological terms: ears opened, tongue loosened (elythe ho desmos tes glosses autou — the bond/chain of his tongue was released), and he spoke plainly (elalei orthos). The "bond of the tongue" echoes Isaiah 35:5-6: "Then the ears of the deaf will be unstopped… and the mute tongue will shout for joy" — a passage describing the restoration accompanying God's coming kingdom. The crowd's response in v.37 will explicitly echo this Isaiah text, identifying Jesus's act as eschatological restoration, not merely a humanitarian miracle.
36: The command not to tell anyone follows the Messianic Secret pattern running through Mark 1-9. Mark's ironic observation — the more Jesus commanded silence, the more they spread the word — is the reverse of the disciples' consistent failure to understand: these Gentile outsiders cannot stop proclaiming what Jewish insiders cannot grasp. The paradox is structurally significant in a Gospel that ends with the women's silence at the empty tomb (16:8): silence and speech are distributed with theological deliberateness throughout the narrative.
37: The crowd's astonishment formula "He has done everything well" (kalos panta pepoieken) deliberately echoes LXX Genesis 1:31 ("God saw everything that he had made, and behold it was very good"). Mark's specific phrasing connects Jesus's healing acts to creation language — these are acts of new creation, not merely medical interventions. The specific mention of the deaf hearing and the mute speaking fulfills Isaiah 35:5-6, identifying the healing as eschatological restoration. The Gentile crowd's acclaim closes the Gentile circuit (7:24-8:10) before the second feeding miracle.
